did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with ResNet-152 and AlexNet architectures to classify skin cancer types (benign vs. malignant) using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The study evaluated model performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score to obtain results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing the performance of ResNet-152 and AlexNet architectures for skin cancer classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It includes specific sections on methodology (data preprocessing, dataset splitting, hyperparameter tuning), implementation details (tools, optimizers), and quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices), which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: ANALISIS PERFORMA RESNET-152 DAN ALEXNET DALAM KLASIFIKASI JENIS KANKER KULIT
journal: Satuan Tulisan Riset dan Inovasi Teknologi
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) using ResNet-152 and AlexNet architectures for classifying skin cancer into benign and malignant categories. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset, which contained 23,257 images after preprocessing and balancing steps. Models were trained using two optimizers, Adam and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD), with a learning rate of 0.0001. Performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics derived from confusion matrices. The results indicated that the ResNet-152 architecture combined with the MBGD optimizer achieved the best performance. Specifically, this configuration yielded an accuracy of 87.85%, outperforming the AlexNet models.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 8 specific lesion types in the ISIC 2019 dataset used in the study. Grouped into the 'Benign' class for the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer into benign and malignant classes
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of skin cancer types into 'benign' and 'malignant' categories using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The models (ResNet-152 and AlexNet) predict discrete diagnostic labels. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score derived from confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archives, accessed via https://challenge.isic-archive.com/landing/2019/. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training ResNet-152 and AlexNet models after balancing the class distribution (specifically reducing NV samples) and preprocessing (resizing to 224x224, removing padding/noise).
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning (batch size and epoch selection) and validation during training.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) of the trained models.
use.other: The original dataset contained 25,331 images, but 2,074 images were removed during preprocessing (including downsampled images), resulting in the final count of 23,257.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparison was based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) derived from confusion matrices.

ISIC 2019.notes: The ResNet-152 architecture combined with the MBGD optimizer achieved the best performance. The paper notes that ResNet-152 generally had higher accuracy than AlexNet, and MBGD tended to perform better than Adam for this specific task.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-152 won the ILSVRC 2015 competition with a top-5 error rate of 3.6%. AlexNet won the ILSVRC 2012 competition.

ResNet-152.structure: Five main convolution processes with many convolution blocks and one fully connected layer. Uses skip connections (residual logic) to prevent gradient loss in deep networks.

AlexNet.structure: 5 convolutional layers with varying kernel sizes and filters, and 3 fully connected layers.

training_details.data_balancing: Balancing performed by reducing samples of the Melanocytic Nevus (NV) class to address class imbalance.
training_details.preprocessing: Removal of duplicate images, removal of padding/noise, resizing to 224x224, and removal of downsampled images.

preprocessing_techniques: Removal of padding to reduce noise
Class balancing by reducing samples of the Melanocytic Nevus (NV) class

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report the use of data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, or synthetic data generation) during training. The text explicitly notes that validation accuracy and loss were unstable because the validation data was more diverse than the training data, which 'sudah diterapkan data augmentation pada proses pelatihannya' (had already applied data augmentation in its training process). However, no specific augmentation methods, probabilities, or settings are detailed in the provided text.

conclusions: The ResNet-152 architecture combined with the Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD) optimizer achieved the best performance for classifying skin cancer types (benign vs. malignant) using the ISIC 2019 dataset.
The optimal model configuration (ResNet-152 with MBGD) achieved an accuracy of 87.85%, outperforming the AlexNet architectures and the ResNet-152 model trained with the Adam optimizer.
ResNet-152 generally demonstrated higher accuracy compared to AlexNet in this binary classification task.
The Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD) optimizer tended to perform better than the Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam) optimizer for this specific task and dataset.
Validation accuracy and loss curves were unstable for both architectures, attributed to the validation data being more diverse than the training data, which had undergone data augmentation.
important_remarks: The study performs binary classification (benign vs. malignant) by aggregating 8 specific ISIC 2019 lesion types, which may obscure class-specific performance nuances compared to multi-class classification.
Class imbalance was addressed by manually reducing samples of the Melanocytic Nevus (NV) class rather than using standard augmentation or weighting techniques, potentially introducing selection bias.

Validation accuracy and loss curves were reported as unstable for both architectures, attributed to the validation data being more diverse than the training data, which limits the reliability of hyperparameter tuning based on validation metrics.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance differences between models were determined solely by descriptive metrics.

The dataset was preprocessed by removing 'downsampled' images and padding, reducing the original 25,331 images to 23,257, but the specific criteria for removing 'noise' or 'duplicate' images are not fully detailed.
The study claims data augmentation was applied to the training data to explain validation instability, but no specific augmentation techniques, parameters, or probabilities are reported in the text.

The best performing model (ResNet-152 with MBGD) achieved an accuracy of 87.85%, which is moderate for skin cancer classification tasks and may not be sufficient for high-stakes clinical deployment without further validation.
The research was conducted using Google Colab and Kaggle environments, which may introduce variability in hardware specifications (e.g., GPU type) affecting reproducibility.
